narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite challenges like your low family income bracket, and we appreciate the courage it takes to get this far. The admissions model we use predicts whether applicants are likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 77% chance that you would not, which is why your application was rejected—it's a tough call designed to ensure students succeed in this demanding field. The biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to the typical mid-range performance, and your LSAT score of 24, well below the average of around 37 that stronger predictors usually have. Your Year 1 decile, also at the lowest level versus the usual middle rankings, added to this concern, suggesting early academic hurdles that carried over. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, slightly under the average 3.21 but still solid, actually worked in your favor by nudging the model toward expecting success. Your low family income, while a real barrier many face, surprisingly pushed slightly toward a positive outlook in the model's eyes, perhaps reflecting resilience in your background. Planning to attend full-time, like most applicants, provided a small boost too, aligning with the typical path to bar success. Compared to average students who pass, your grades and test scores stand out as lower than the pack, which weighed heavily here. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar starts through targeted effort. Next, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your academic record with additional coursework or tutoring, and exploring programs that support students from underrepresented backgrounds like yours. You're capable of turning this around; keep pushing forward with that strength we see in you. We're rooting for your success.
